title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Great Fire of London. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: London’s Rebirth: The Great Fire of 1666
content.text: In September 1666, London was a city made of wood and straw, a tinderbox waiting for a spark. After a long, dry summer, the city was parched, and the narrow, crowded streets were filled with flammable materials like pitch and tallow. The disaster began in a small bakery on Pudding Lane, where a spark from an oven was fanned by an unusually strong east wind. ||(41)|| [41]Within hours, the fire had leapt from the bakery to the surrounding warehouses, which were filled with oil, brandy, and coal[/41]. The heat was so intense that it created its own weather system, sucking in oxygen and creating a firestorm that was impossible to contain using the primitive firefighting tools of the era. ||(42)|| [42]This lack of effective response was compounded by a delay in decision-making[/42]. The Lord Mayor, Thomas Bloodworth, initially dismissed the fire as a minor incident, famously claiming it was too small to be a threat. By the time he realized the scale of the danger, the flames had already consumed several blocks and were moving toward the heart of the city. ||(43)|| [43]The only way to stop the advance was to create 'firebreaks'—the deliberate destruction of houses in the path of the flames to starve the fire of fuel[/43]. However, this was a controversial move, as it meant destroying the property of healthy citizens to save the rest of the city. It wasn't until King Charles II took personal charge of the operations that the demolition began in earnest. ||(44)|| [44]After four days, the wind finally died down, and the fire was brought under control[/44]. The statistics were staggering: 13,000 houses, 87 parish churches, and the magnificent St. Paul's Cathedral had been reduced to ash. Amazingly, despite the massive destruction of property, only six deaths were officially recorded, though modern historians suspect the true toll was much higher. ||(45)|| [45]From the ruins emerged a new vision for the city[/45]. Sir Christopher Wren, among others, proposed a radical redesign of London with wide boulevards and geometric plazas. While the old property lines ultimately forced a more traditional street plan, the new building regulations ensured that the city would never burn like that again. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the Monument to the Great Fire stands near where it all began[/46]. It serves as a reminder of a city that refused to die, a place that used a catastrophe as an opportunity to reinvent itself into a modern global capital. The fire was a tragedy, but it was also the crucible in which the modern London was forged.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the timing of the outbreak [/41] could not have been worse. The wind was blowing the flames directly into the densely packed wooden structures of the riverside, where the proximity of the buildings allowed the fire to jump easily from roof to roof.
paragraphs.B: Because [42] there was no organized fire brigade [/42], citizens relied on 'fire buckets' and hand-pumped squirts that could only throw a small amount of water. These were useless against a blaze that was melting the lead off the roofs of churches and turning the cobblestones red-hot.
paragraphs.C: As [43] the panic spread [/43], the narrow streets became clogged with people trying to flee with their possessions. Many threw their furniture into the Thames, while others paid exorbitant prices to carters to carry their belongings to the open fields of Islington or Highgate.
paragraphs.D: This [44] drastic strategy of demolition [/44] eventually worked. By blowing up entire rows of houses with gunpowder, the King’s guards created a gap that the fire could not cross, finally halting its progress toward the Tower of London and the wealthy West End.
paragraphs.E: Beyond [45] the immediate physical loss [/45], the fire had a surprising benefit: it likely ended the Great Plague that had decimated the city the previous year. By destroying the rat-infested slums and the bacteria-laden wooden pipes, the fire unintentionally sanitized the urban environment.
paragraphs.F: This [46] legislative legacy [/46] required all new houses to be built of brick or stone, with thicker walls and wider streets to prevent the spread of flames. These rules not only made the city safer but also gave London its characteristic red-brick appearance that survives to this day.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] conspiracy theories initially emerged [/47] regarding the cause of the fire. In a city already tense from war and religious conflict, many Londoners were quick to blame foreign spies or Catholic plotters, leading to a wave of xenophobia and several wrongful arrests.

questions.explanation: A refers to the 'timing of the outbreak' and the wind direction, which explains why the fire from the bakery was able to jump to the warehouses as described in the text.
B identifies the 'lack of an organized fire brigade' and the 'primitive tools', linking directly to the 'lack of effective response' mentioned after the gap.
C describes the 'panic' and the movement of the people, which fits the narrative of the fire 'moving toward the heart of the city' and the chaos that ensued.
D refers to the 'drastic strategy of demolition' (firebreaks) using gunpowder, which explains how King Charles II's actions finally stopped the fire.
E explains a 'surprising benefit' (ending the Plague) that resulted from the 'massive destruction of property' and the loss of houses mentioned before the gap.
F refers to the 'legislative legacy' (building regulations) that followed the fire, explaining how the new city was made safer as mentioned in the text.
